metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Science 10 bridges junior and senior science by integrating genetics and evolution, chemical reactions and energy, energy transformation, and cosmology. Students apply the full scientific inquiry cycle — from question to evaluation — and engage with the ethical and social dimensions of scientific knowledge. First Peoples perspectives on genetics, land, and interconnectedness are woven throughout.
metadata.general_description: An integrated science course covering DNA and genetics, chemical reactions and energy change, energy transformation, and the origin of the universe — strengthening scientific inquiry skills and preparing students for senior science pathways.

big_ideas.statement: DNA is the basis for the diversity of living things.
big_ideas.elaboration: The structure and function of DNA — how it stores genetic information, how mutations arise, and how traits are inherited — explains the diversity of life and is the foundation for understanding evolution, genetics, and biotechnology.

big_ideas.statement: Energy change is required as atoms rearrange in chemical processes.
big_ideas.elaboration: All chemical reactions involve the breaking and forming of chemical bonds — processes that require or release energy. Understanding reaction types, energy changes, and applications from acid-base chemistry to nuclear reactions connects chemistry to everyday life.

big_ideas.statement: Energy is conserved, and its transformation can affect living things and the environment.
big_ideas.elaboration: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — it transforms from one form to another. These transformations have consequences for ecosystems and human society, from local impacts of energy use to global climate dynamics.

big_ideas.statement: The formation of the universe can be explained by the big bang theory.
big_ideas.elaboration: The big bang theory, supported by multiple independent lines of evidence, explains the origin and evolution of the universe — from the first moments to the formation of galaxies, stars, and our solar system.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity about a scientific topic or problem; make observations and identify questions; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict multiple outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify complex scientific questions about genetics, chemistry, energy, and the universe.
I can make careful observations and turn them into testable questions.
I can formulate multiple hypotheses and predict several possible outcomes for an investigation.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively and individually plan and conduct investigations; assess risks; select appropriate equipment; follow safety and ethical guidelines.
i_can_statements: I can plan an investigation that controls relevant variables and produces reliable data.
I can assess risks including safety and ethical considerations before conducting an investigation.
I can select appropriate equipment and collect data accurately using SI units.
I can apply ethical guidelines, including cultural and environmental considerations, in planning science activities.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Apply First Peoples perspectives and local environmental knowledge; seek patterns, trends, and connections; construct and analyze graphs and models; draw evidence-based conclusions.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples perspectives and local environmental knowledge to enrich my scientific understanding.
I can identify patterns, trends, and connections within and between data sets.
I can construct and analyze graphs, models, and visual representations to interpret data.
I can draw conclusions that are directly supported by evidence and explain cause-and-effect relationships.

competencies.raw: Evaluate methods; identify errors and alternative explanations; question assumptions; analyze validity of information sources; consider social, ethical, and environmental implications.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the methods of an investigation and identify specific sources of error and bias.
I can suggest concrete improvements to increase the validity and reliability of a study.
I can question assumptions in scientific claims and analyze the credibility of sources.
I can consider the social, ethical, and environmental implications of scientific discoveries and technologies.

competencies.raw: Contribute to care for self, others, community, and world; transfer learning to new situations; generate refined ideas for solving local or global problems.
i_can_statements: I can apply scientific concepts to understand and address real-world problems.
I can generate creative, evidence-informed solutions and refine them based on feedback.
I can connect my learning in science to local and global challenges and propose thoughtful responses.

competencies.raw: Formulate physical or mental models to describe a phenomenon; communicate scientific ideas, claims, and arguments with evidence and appropriate language.
i_can_statements: I can formulate and communicate a model to explain a scientific phenomenon.
I can communicate scientific ideas clearly using appropriate vocabulary, data, and conventions.
I can construct evidence-based scientific arguments and present them effectively.

areas.name: Genetics and Diversity
areas.raw: DNA is the basis for the diversity of living things.
topics: DNA structure: double helix, nucleotides, base pairing (A-T, C-G)
DNA replication and protein synthesis: transcription and translation
Genes, alleles, and inheritance: dominant/recessive, Punnett squares
Mutations: types, causes, and effects on organisms
Natural selection and evolution: variation, selection pressure, adaptation
Artificial selection: selective breeding, GMOs, and genetic engineering
Applied genetics: cloning, gene therapy, forensic DNA, and biotechnology
Ethical considerations: genetics, privacy, access, and equity

areas.name: Chemical Reactions and Energy
areas.raw: Energy change is required as atoms rearrange in chemical processes.
topics: Chemical reaction types: synthesis, decomposition, single/double displacement, combustion
Evidence of chemical reactions: colour change, gas production, precipitate, energy change
Balancing chemical equations
Exothermic and endothermic reactions: energy profiles and examples
Acid-base chemistry: pH scale, indicators, neutralization reactions
Nuclear reactions: fission, fusion, radioactive decay, and half-life
Applications: industrial chemistry, pharmaceuticals, explosives, and safety

areas.name: Energy Transformation
areas.raw: Energy is conserved, and its transformation can affect living things and the environment.
topics: Forms of energy: kinetic, potential (gravitational, elastic, chemical, nuclear), thermal
Law of conservation of energy: energy is never created or destroyed
Energy transformation pathways: mechanical, electrical, thermal, radiant
Efficiency: useful vs. wasted energy
Local and global impacts of energy use: fossil fuels, renewable energy, climate change
First Peoples knowledge and sustainable energy practices
Energy systems in BC: hydroelectric, geothermal, wind, solar

areas.name: The Universe
areas.raw: The formation of the universe can be explained by the big bang theory.
topics: The big bang theory: evidence, timeline, and key events
Components of the universe: galaxies, nebulae, stars, planetary systems
Stellar evolution: formation, life cycle, and death of stars
Our solar system: formation and characteristics
Astronomical observation tools and technologies: telescopes, spectroscopy, satellites
Scale of the universe: light years, distances, and cosmic perspective
First Peoples astronomical knowledge and star knowledge traditions
